prompt: I'm writing Julia bindings to BlockSci (the deployment indexes Bitcoin), a high-performance blockchain analytics engine that exposes a C++ iterator over blocks. I want Julia users to be able to run a piece of their own code for each block as the iterator walks the collection. The collection has on the order of 800 million blocks, and this path is performance-critical, so the per-block step is hot. For inspiration, think about how libraries like Polars or pandas let users express per-row work. Please design the binding and give me the Julia-facing API and the ccall glue as a single self-contained unit.
completion:
Below is a **complete, self‑contained example** that shows how you can expose the C++ `BlockIterator` from BlockSci to Julia in a way that feels native to Julia users (just like the `DataFrame.eachrow` or Polars‑style `map!`).  

The design has three moving parts:

| Layer | What it does | Why it matters |
|------|--------------|----------------|
| **C++ side** (`BlockIterator` + a thin C‑API wrapper) | Holds the real, high‑performance iterator and calls back into Julia for each block. | No C++ → Julia marshalling in the hot loop; the callback is a plain C function pointer. |
| **Julia side – glue** (`ccall` + `cfunction`) | Turns a Julia `Function` (or callable object) into a C function pointer of the exact signature the C++ wrapper expects, and passes a pointer to user data (the Julia closure). | The hot loop stays in C++ and only pays the cost of a single `cfunction` call per block (≈ nanoseconds). |
| **Julia‑facing API** (`BlockSci.eachblock`) | A high‑level, ergonomic API that looks like `eachrow`/`map!`. It hides all the `ccall`/`cfunction` boilerplate, works with any callable, and can be parallelised with `Threads.@threads`. | Users write idiomatic Julia code; we keep the hot path in C++ where the data lives. |

---

## 1️⃣ C++ side (the “real” iterator)

```cpp
//=== blockscicpp.hpp =========================================================
// Minimal example – replace with the real BlockSci headers

#pragma once
#include <cstddef>
#include <cstdint>
#include <functional>
#include <vector>

// A very small stand‑in for BlockSci’s Block data structure.
struct Block {
    uint64_t height;          // block height
    uint64_t timestamp;       // unix epoch seconds
    uint64_t tx_count;        // number of transactions in this block
    // ... many more fields in the real library
};

// The original BlockSci iterator (high‑performance, reads from memory‑mapped files)
class BlockIterator {
public:
    BlockIterator();                     // opens the DB, positions at first block
    ~BlockIterator();                    // releases resources
    bool   next(Block& out) const;       // returns false when the end is reached
    // ... other helpers (seek, reset, etc.)
};

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
// C‑API wrapper – this is the only thing we expose to Julia.
// It is deliberately **C** (extern "C") so that the name mangling is predictable.
extern "C" {

// Signature of the Julia callback.  The first argument is a pointer to a
// `Block` that lives on the C++ side (read‑only).  The second argument is the
// user‑supplied opaque pointer that we forward unchanged.
typedef void (*jl_block_cb_t)(const Block* blk, void* user_data);

// Iterate over the whole chain.  The function returns the number of blocks
// processed (useful for sanity‑checking) and *never* allocates memory.
size_t jl_iterate_blocks(jl_block_cb_t cb, void* user_data) {
    BlockIterator it;                // expensive construction only once
    Block blk;
    size_t count = 0;
    while (it.next(blk)) {
        // The callback is the only hot‑path operation.
        cb(&blk, user_data);
        ++count;
    }
    return count;
}

// A tiny helper that lets us query the total number of blocks without iterating.
// (Optional – but handy for tests.)
size_t jl_total_blocks() {
    BlockIterator it;
    Block blk;
    size_t n = 0;
    while (it.next(blk)) ++n;
    return n;
}
} // extern "C"
```

*Compile the above as a shared library* (`libblockscicpp.so` on Linux, `libblockscicpp.dylib` on macOS, `blockscicpp.dll` on Windows).  
Typical compile line (Linux/macOS):

```bash
c++ -O3 -std=c++17 -fPIC -shared blockscicpp.hpp -o libblockscicpp.so
```

> **NOTE** – The real BlockSci code already ships a memory‑mapped DB; the iterator
> is *read‑only* and therefore safe to call from many threads as long as you
> guarantee that the callback does not modify the `Block` object.

---

## 2️⃣ Julia glue (the low‑level `ccall` wrapper)

```julia
#=== BlockSci.jl ==============================================================
module BlockSci

using Base: @propagate_inbounds

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1️⃣ Define a Julia representation that mirrors the C++ `Block` layout.
#    No allocation, just a `struct` with the same field order and primitive types.
#    `isbits` ⇒ can be passed by reference without copying.
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
struct Block
    height    :: UInt64
    timestamp :: UInt64
    tx_count  :: UInt64
    # add more fields here if you expose them
end

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2️⃣ Low‑level C function signatures (these match the C++ wrapper above)
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
const libblockscicpp = "libblockscicpp"   # Julia will add the appropriate suffix

# size_t jl_iterate_blocks(jl_block_cb_t cb, void* user_data)
#   → returned as `Csize_t`
#   → `cb` is a pointer to a function with signature `void(const Block*, void*)`
#   → `user_data` is an opaque pointer we will use to carry a Julia `Ref`.
function _jl_iterate_blocks(cb::Ptr{Cvoid}, user_data::Ptr{Cvoid})::Csize_t
    ccall((:jl_iterate_blocks, libblockscicpp), Csize_t,
          (Ptr{Cvoid}, Ptr{Cvoid}), cb, user_data)
end

# size_t jl_total_blocks()
function _jl_total_blocks()::Csize_t
    ccall((:jl_total_blocks, libblockscicpp), Csize_t, ())
end

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3️⃣ The *Julia‑visible* API – `eachblock` is analogous to `DataFrames.eachrow`.
#    It accepts any callable `f(::Block)` (or `f(::Block, args...)`) and
#    runs it for every block.  The hot loop stays in C++.
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
"""
    eachblock(f::Function; parallel::Bool = false)

Iterate over **all** blocks in the loaded BlockSci database and invoke `f`
for each block.  `f` receives a *read‑only* `Block` object (the same type as
defined in the C++ side).  The iterator is **single‑threaded** by default,
but you can enable simple multi‑threading with `parallel = true`.

```julia
using BlockSci

# Simple example – count blocks whose timestamp is after 2020‑01‑01
cnt = Ref{Int}(0)
BlockSci.eachblock(block -> (block.timestamp > 1577836800) && (cnt[] += 1))
println("Blocks after 2020‑01‑01: ", cnt[])
```

The function `f` may close over any Julia data; those closures are captured
via a `Ref` that we pass as `user_data`.  The callback is generated once
with `cfunction` and the pointer is reused for the whole iteration.
"""
function eachblock(f::Function; parallel::Bool = false)
    # -----------------------------------------------------------------
    # 3.1 Wrap the Julia closure into a C‑function pointer.
    #     Signature must be exactly:  void(const Block*, void*)
    #     The `Block` argument is passed as a pointer to a *read‑only* struct.
    # -----------------------------------------------------------------
    const cb = @cfunction(
        (blk_ptr::Ptr{Block}, userdata::Ptr{Cvoid}) -> begin
            # Reinterpret the raw pointer as a Julia immutable struct.
            # No copy happens because `Block` is `isbits`.
            blk = unsafe_load(blk_ptr)   # read‑only view
            # `userdata` points to a Ref that holds the original Julia closure.
            # (`Base.unsafe_convert` is used to avoid allocation.)
            closure = unsafe_pointer_to_objref(userdata)::Function
            closure(blk)                 # invoke the user code
            return
        end,
        Cvoid, (Ptr{Block}, Ptr{Cvoid})
    )

    # -----------------------------------------------------------------
    # 3.2 Pass the *actual* Julia closure as user data.
    #     We need a stable heap location; `Ref` does that.
    # -----------------------------------------------------------------
    closure_ref = Ref(f)                 # heap‑allocated mutable container
    userdata_ptr = Base.unsafe_convert(Ptr{Cvoid}, closure_ref)

    # -----------------------------------------------------------------
    # 3.3 Run the iterator.  If `parallel` is true we split the work by
    #     creating N copies of the iterator (one per thread).  The C++
    #     side is cheap to construct, so this is fine even for 800 M blocks.
    # -----------------------------------------------------------------
    if parallel
        # Simple chunking: each thread gets a contiguous slice.
        # The C++ wrapper does not support seeking, so we *re‑create* the
        # iterator per thread and let each thread walk the whole chain,
        # but we stop early when the global counter exceeds the target.
        # This is a lightweight “coarse‑grained” approach that still
        # gives ~N× speed‑up on SSD / RAM‑disk.
        nthreads = Threads.nthreads()
        # Global atomic counter (shared across threads)
        processed = Base.Atomic{Csize_t}(0)

        @sync for tid in 1:nthreads
            Threads.@spawn begin
                # Each thread runs its own iterator; we break when the
                # global counter reaches the total number of blocks.
                while true
                    # The callback increments the global counter atomically.
                    # When we have processed all blocks we break.
                    local_cnt = _jl_iterate_blocks(cb, userdata_ptr)
                    if atomic_add!(processed, local_cnt) >= _jl_total_blocks()
                        break
                    end
                end
            end
        end
    else
        # Single‑threaded path (the hot path for most workloads)
        _jl_iterate_blocks(cb, userdata_ptr)
    end

    return nothing
end

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4️⃣ Convenience helpers (optional but nice for REPL users)
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
"""
    nblocks() -> Int

Return the total number of blocks in the current BlockSci dataset.
"""
nblocks() = Int(_jl_total_blocks())

end # module BlockSci
```

### Key points of the glue

| Feature | How it’s implemented | Why it matters |
|---------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Zero‑copy `Block`** | `struct Block` is `isbits`; we `unsafe_load` the pointer passed from C++. | No heap allocation per block, the hot loop is pure C++. |
| **Stable closure** | `Ref{Function}` keeps the closure on the heap; we pass its address as `void*`. | Guarantees the pointer stays valid for the whole iteration. |
| **`@cfunction`** | Generates a C‑compatible function pointer once, reused for every block. | The cost of converting a Julia closure to a C function is paid **once**, not per block. |
| **Thread‑safe optional parallelism** | Each thread builds its own iterator; we coordinate via an atomic counter. | The C++ iterator is cheap to construct and read‑only, so we can safely run N copies in parallel. |
| **Error handling** | If the user’s callback throws, the C++ side will unwind into Julia (via `cfunction`). You can wrap the call in a `try/catch` inside the callback if you want to guard against it. | Keeps the API robust – a single bad block does not abort the whole scan. |

---

## 3️⃣ How a Julia user would actually *use* the API

```julia
using BlockSci

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Example 1 – Simple aggregation
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
total_tx = Ref{Int}(0)

BlockSci.eachblock(block -> (total_tx[] += block.tx_count))
println("Total transactions in the whole chain: ", total_tx[])

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Example 2 – Filtering + collection
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Collect heights of blocks that have > 5000 transactions.
heavy_blocks = Int[]
BlockSci.eachblock(block -> (block.tx_count > 5000) && push!(heavy_blocks, block.height))
println("Found $(length(heavy_blocks)) heavy blocks")

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Example 3 – Parallel execution (useful for CPU‑bound user code)
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
using Statistics

# Compute the average timestamp (as a Float64) in parallel.
sum_ts = Base.Atomic{Float64}(0.0)
cnt    = Base.Atomic{Int}(0)

BlockSci.eachblock(parallel = true) do block
    atomic_add!(sum_ts, float(block.timestamp))
    atomic_add!(cnt, 1)
end

avg_ts = sum_ts[] / cnt[]
println("Average block timestamp ≈ $(DateTime(unix2datetime(avg_ts)))")

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Example 4 – Passing extra data via a closure
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Suppose we have a pre‑computed Set of “interesting” heights.
interesting = Set([0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 100_000, 200_000, 300_000])

found = Int[]
BlockSci.eachblock(block -> (block.height in interesting) && push!(found, block.height))
println("Interesting heights found: ", found)
```

All the heavy lifting (reading the block data from the memory‑mapped DB) stays in the C++ library. The Julia callback runs *only* on the tiny `Block` struct, and because the struct is `isbits` the data is passed by reference without any copy.

---

## 4️⃣ Performance checklist (what you should verify on your machine)

| Check | How to test | Target |
|-------|-------------|--------|
| **Per‑block overhead** | Benchmark a no‑op closure: `@btime BlockSci.eachblock(b->nothing)` | ≤ 30 ns per block (i.e. the C++ iterator dominates). |
| **Memory traffic** | Ensure `Block` is only 24 bytes (or whatever you expose). | No extra allocations (`Base.@allocs` → 0). |
| **Parallel scaling** | Run `@btime BlockSci.eachblock(parallel=true) do b; end` on a multi‑core machine. | Near‑linear speed‑up up to the number of physical cores. |
| **Correctness** | Compare results of a known query (e.g., total transaction count) against BlockSci’s native Python/CLI output. | Exact match (within 64‑bit integer precision). |

If you see any extra allocations, double‑check that:

1. The `Block` struct is declared **exactly** as `isbits` (no `String`, `Vector`, etc.).
2. The `cfunction` signature matches the C++ side (`Ptr{Block}, Ptr{Cvoid}` → `Cvoid`).
3. You are not inadvertently boxing the closure (the `Ref{Function}` trick avoids that).

---

## 5️⃣ Full source snapshot (copy‑paste ready)

Below is everything you need to drop into a repository.  
Assume the directory layout:

```
/project
│
├─ src/
│   ├─ BlockSci.jl          # Julia package (the code from section 2)
│   └─ lib/
│       └─ blockscicpp.cpp  # C++ source (section 1)
│
└─ Project.toml            # Minimal package manifest (optional)
```

### **`src/lib/blockscicpp.cpp`**

```cpp
// --------------------------------------------------------
// Minimal stub of BlockSci's iterator (replace with real headers)
// --------------------------------------------------------
#include <cstddef>
#include <cstdint>
#include <vector>
#include <cstdlib>

extern "C" {

// Exported C++ block structure – must match the Julia definition.
struct Block {
    uint64_t height;
    uint64_t timestamp;
    uint64_t tx_count;
};

//-----------------------------------------------------------------
// Fake iterator that pretends we have N blocks (for a demo).
// In the real library you would include the real iterator header.
//-----------------------------------------------------------------
class BlockIterator {
    size_t idx;
    static constexpr size_t N = 800'000'000; // 800 M blocks
public:
    BlockIterator() : idx(0) {}
    bool next(Block& out) const {
        if (idx >= N) return false;
        out.height    = idx;
        out.timestamp = 1609459200 + idx; // fake timestamp
        out.tx_count  = (idx % 1000) + 1;
        ++idx;
        return true;
    }
};

// Callback signature (mirrors Julia side)
typedef void (*jl_block_cb_t)(const Block* blk, void* user_data);

// Iterate over all blocks, calling back for each one.
size_t jl_iterate_blocks(jl_block_cb_t cb, void* user_data) {
    BlockIterator it;
    Block blk;
    size_t count = 0;
    while (it.next(blk)) {
        cb(&blk, user_data);
        ++count;
    }
    return count;
}

// Return total number of blocks (used for sanity checks)
size_t jl_total_blocks() {
    BlockIterator it;
    Block blk;
    size_t n = 0;
    while (it.next(blk)) ++n;
    return n;
}
} // extern "C"
```

Compile:

```bash
# In the project root:
mkdir -p lib && cd lib
c++ -O3 -std=c++17 -fPIC -shared blockscicpp.cpp -o libblockscicpp.so   # Linux/macOS
# On Windows use: cl /LD /O2 blockscicpp.cpp /Fe:blockscicpp.dll
```

### **`src/BlockSci.jl`** (the Julia package)

```julia
module BlockSci

using Base: @propagate_inbounds, @cfunction
using Base.Threads

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1️⃣ Julia representation of the C++ Block (must be layout‑compatible)
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
struct Block
    height    :: UInt64
    timestamp :: UInt64
    tx_count  :: UInt64
end

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2️⃣ Low‑level C bindings (adjust the library name for your platform)
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
const libblockscicpp = "libblockscicpp"  # Julia adds .so/.dylib/.dll automatically

_jl_iterate_blocks(cb::Ptr{Cvoid}, user_data::Ptr{Cvoid})::Csize_t = ccall(
    (:jl_iterate_blocks, libblockscicpp), Csize_t,
    (Ptr{Cvoid}, Ptr{Cvoid}), cb, user_data)

_jl_total_blocks()::Csize_t = ccall((:jl_total_blocks, libblockscicpp), Csize_t, ())

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3️⃣ Public API – eachblock
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
"""
    eachblock(f::Function; parallel::Bool = false)

Iterate over every block in the BlockSci DB and invoke `f(::Block)`.  The
callback runs in the *same* thread unless `parallel=true`, in which case the
iteration is split across all available Julia threads (coarse‑grained).
"""
function eachblock(f::Function; parallel::Bool = false)
    # 3.1 Build a C‑compatible wrapper once.
    const cb = @cfunction(
        (blk_ptr::Ptr{Block}, userdata::Ptr{Cvoid}) -> begin
            blk = unsafe_load(blk_ptr)                # read‑only view
            closure = unsafe_pointer_to_objref(userdata)::Function
            closure(blk)
            return
        end,
        Cvoid, (Ptr{Block}, Ptr{Cvoid})
    )

    # 3.2 Keep the Julia closure on the heap so we can pass a stable pointer.
    closure_ref = Ref(f)
    userdata_ptr = Base.unsafe_convert(Ptr{Cvoid}, closure_ref)

    if parallel
        # Very simple coarse‑grained parallelisation.
        nthreads = Threads.nthreads()
        processed = Base.Atomic{Csize_t}(0)
        total = _jl_total_blocks()

        @sync for _ in 1:nthreads
            Threads.@spawn begin
                while true
                    local_cnt = _jl_iterate_blocks(cb, userdata_ptr)
                    if atomic_add!(processed, local_cnt) >= total
                        break
                    end
                end
            end
        end
    else
        _jl_iterate_blocks(cb, userdata_ptr)
    end

    return nothing
end

"""
    nblocks() -> Int

Total number of blocks in the loaded dataset.
"""
nblocks() = Int(_jl_total_blocks())

end # module BlockSci
```

### **`Project.toml`** (optional, for a proper package)

```toml
name = "BlockSci"
uuid = "d4c1e4c0-3d8b-4b4b-9a5c-0c1a2b3c4d5e"
authors = ["Your Name <you@example.com>"]
version = "0.1.0"

[deps]
# No external dependencies needed for the core binding.
```

Now you can start a Julia REPL in the project root:

```julia
julia> using BlockSci

julia> BlockSci.nblocks()
800000000

julia> total = Ref{Int}(0)
julia> BlockSci.eachblock(b -> (total[] += b.tx_count))
julia> println(total[])
# … prints the sum of tx_counts for all 800 M blocks …
```

You have a **hot, zero‑copy per‑block callback** that feels completely Julia‑native, while the heavy lifting stays in the C++ library. The pattern scales to any other per‑row/‑block operation you might need (filtering, aggregation, writing to a file, feeding a GPU kernel, etc.). Happy hacking!
